objective.question: How do LPBF/SLM process parameters, thermal history, porosity, texture, and post-treatment affect densification, microstructure, mechanical, fatigue, corrosion, and wear behavior of 316L stainless steel?

required_paper_contributions.must_explain: Energy density, scanning speed, and scanning strategy jointly affect densification, microstructure, and mechanical properties in SLM 316L.
150 C build-platform preheating changes cooling rate, melt-pool geometry, porosity, and tensile response.
Volumetric energy density changes density, defect size, grain and melt-pool structure, tensile behavior, and fatigue behavior.
Heat treatment and HIP reduce residual stress and improve elongation but reduce yield strength or hardness, and do not guarantee full pore closure.
Porosity level and pore type affect mechanical trends and corrosion resistance through pitting and passive-film stability.
Scan rotation angle and build orientation affect crystallographic texture and yield-strength prediction.

required_claims.text: LPBF/SLM 316L behavior is controlled by coupled energy input, scan strategy, thermal history, post-treatment, porosity, texture, and test environment rather than one process parameter alone.
P001 S014 is the strongest within-paper sample across relative density, yield strength, ultimate tensile strength, elongation, and microhardness.
P002 150 C preheating lowers cooling rate, increases melt-pool width/depth ratio, and improves yield strength and elongation while ultimate tensile strength changes little.

P003 higher VED improves density and reduces maximum defect diameter, but the high-VED printed sample has lower yield and ultimate tensile strength than low-VED while showing higher total elongation and higher low-cycle fatigue strength.
P003 printed samples have fatigue limits around 80-100 MPa, far below wrought 316L at about 256 MPa.
P004 heat treatment and HIP reduce residual stress and improve elongation, but reduce hardness and yield strength relative to as-SLM under the same 120 W, 100 mm/s condition.
required_claims.source_hint: P004 HT/HIP comparison at 120 W and 100 mm/s

required_claims.text: P005 135 W-750 mm/s has the best corrosion resistance among the three SLM samples, with the highest pitting potential and passive-film resistance.

P006 shows scan rotation angle and build orientation change crystallographic texture and affect yield-strength prediction, while Case 12 remains uncertain because its ODF correlation is low and Jeffrey distance is high.
required_claims.source_hint: P006 ODF correlation and Jeffrey distance table

path: porosity, LoF defects, balling, cellular or dendritic structure
scan rotation angle and build orientation

required_limitations.text: P001 scan-speed units may be uncertain and should be rechecked before recalculation.
P001 relative density is SEM/ImageJ based and should not be treated as equivalent to Archimedes or micro-CT density.
P003 printed-vs-wrought comparisons have different processing histories and cannot be attributed only to VED.
P005 corrosion conclusions are limited to room-temperature 3.5 wt.% NaCl testing.
P006 is primarily a texture and yield-strength prediction paper, not a porosity-defect paper.

forbidden_overclaims.text: Higher energy density is always better for LPBF/SLM 316L.
Density alone explains all mechanical, fatigue, and corrosion behavior.
HIP fully closes all pores in SLM 316L.
P005 corrosion results apply to all corrosion environments.
P006 mainly studies porosity and defect control.
